header.stance: Treasuries cheapen as energy and hawkish Fed repricing lift real yields, with funding orderly
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The July 13 Treasury close extended the restrictive-rate regime from the prior final close, but energy returned as a direct catalyst after having receded from the July 10 move. Renewed US-Iran strikes and the proposed charge on cargo crossing the Strait of Hormuz revived the threat of a fuel-price shock. Christopher Waller then made that shock more potent by tying another hot core-inflation reading to possible near-term tightening. The two-year yield closed at 4.26% and the 10-year at 4.62%, leaving the selloff anchored in a higher expected policy path rather than a flight to safety.

Three channels defined the move. Oil raised the prospective headline-inflation impulse and complicated the path back to price stability. Waller stressed that underlying inflation had already accelerated before the latest energy shock, so traders could not treat the move as a purely geopolitical premium. Money markets lifted the implied chance of a July rate increase to nearly 50%, from less than 40% earlier in the session, making the front end and belly especially sensitive to the next inflation release and Chair Kevin Warsh’s congressional testimony.

The nominal selloff also demanded more real compensation. The 10-year real yield finished at 2.36%, while inflation compensation rose by less than the nominal yield. That composition continued the prior close’s emphasis on restrictive real rates, even as the renewed oil shock added a clearer inflation component. The five-year led the daily cheapening, and the long bond rose less than the belly. Investors were repricing the timing and persistence of policy restraint more forcefully than adding a fresh long-horizon fiscal shock.

Funding and credit supplied the principal limit to a systemic interpretation. Large three- and six-month bill auctions attracted substantial bids, immediate cash-market conditions remained soft, and high-yield listed funds fell much less than technology equities. Yet the cushion was thin: the fiscal deficit and interest bill reinforced the high long-end clearing threshold, AI financing was migrating toward more complex structures, and the dollar strengthened as emerging-market debt weakened. The close therefore combined an inflation-policy shock with orderly absorption, leaving market function intact while raising the cost of duration and refinancing across public and private borrowers.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasuries cheapened across the curve, with the five-year sector underperforming. The two-year yield rose five basis points to 4.26%, the five-year added seven to 4.37%, the 10-year increased six to 4.62%, and the 30-year rose four to 5.10%. Compared with the prior final close, the move extended the front-end reversal and pushed the benchmark and long bond above their July 10 levels. The ordering placed the greatest pressure where an imminent hike and a persistently restrictive path overlap, while the smaller long-bond move showed that fiscal and term premia did not deliver the day’s largest incremental shock.

Curve slopes were mixed because the belly bore much of the adjustment. The 2s10s spread widened one basis point to 36, whereas 2s30s narrowed one to 84. The 2s5s10s butterfly rose three basis points to minus 14, and the 2s10s30s butterfly also rose three to minus 12. A simple bear-flattening label therefore misses the five-year concentration. The front end responded to the prospect of near-term action, the belly incorporated a longer restrictive path, and the 30-year carried a smaller daily increase from an already elevated starting yield.

Inflation-linked Treasuries divided the benchmark move into a dominant real-rate component and a smaller inflation increment. The 10-year real yield rose four basis points to 2.36%, while the 10-year breakeven increased two to 2.26%. Five- and seven-year real yields each gained four basis points, reaching 2.06% and 2.20%. Twenty- and 30-year real yields rose three basis points to 2.69% and 2.89%. Oil lifted inflation concern, but investors also required more real return across the curve as policy tightening became a live near-term possibility.

Waller supplied the reaction-function bridge between energy and real rates. He said another hot core reading could require the FOMC to consider tightening in the near term and noted that core PCE inflation had reached 3.4% in the year through May. His concern extended beyond oil: tariffs, energy and AI infrastructure demand were all contributing to pressure, while consumer demand remained resilient and the labor market appeared stable. He also said several months of lower readings would be needed to establish improvement, raising the bar for a single benign print to reverse the repricing.

The message retained a two-sided boundary. Waller described inflation expectations as well anchored and the labor market as less tight than during the 2021-22 inflation episode. A sustained moderation in core inflation could still justify holding rates steady. Monday’s selloff reflected the asymmetry around the next release: another firm reading could activate a near-term hike discussion, whereas one cooler month would not necessarily settle whether underlying pressure was receding. The approach of Warsh’s first congressional testimony as chair added uncertainty because he had not yet provided a detailed economic view or a rate projection.

The bill curve reinforced the policy interpretation without signaling immediate funding stress. Four- and six-week coupon-equivalent yields each rose one basis point to 3.70%, and the eight-week yield held at 3.72%. The 13-week yield climbed five basis points to 3.85%, the 26-week added four to 4.00%, and the 52-week rose six to 4.12%. The larger repricing from three months to one year aligned with a higher expected policy path; the subdued movement at the shortest maturities was consistent with abundant near-term cash.

Monday’s auctions showed that cash could still absorb large bill supply. The 91-day sale accepted $92.000 billion at a 3.760% high rate and drew 2.84 times as many bids as awards. Indirect bidders received 56.76% of accepted competitive tenders, direct bidders 8.24%, and dealers 34.99%. The 182-day sale accepted $79.001 billion at 3.860%, with 3.11 coverage, a 66.49% indirect share, an 8.09% direct share, and 25.42% going to dealers. Both securities issue and settle on July 16, keeping the auction demand signal separate from the coming cash transfer.

Federal Reserve reserve-management operations offered a second funding counterweight. The New York Fed kept bill purchases at about $10 billion for the monthly period ending August 13 and planned roughly $17.6 billion of reinvestment purchases. Bank reserves stood at $3.14 trillion on July 8, and secured overnight financing had been trading below the rate paid on reserve balances amid abundant cash. Those conditions explain why a hawkish policy repricing could coexist with orderly money markets. The vulnerability lies ahead as Treasury bill issuance and a cash balance expected to exceed $1 trillion withdraw reserves from the banking system.

The rates close separated monetary-policy risk from market plumbing. Energy and Waller pushed expected tightening forward, higher real yields carried most of the benchmark adjustment, and the belly absorbed the largest daily loss. Bill demand and soft secured funding prevented the move from becoming a liquidity event. The long end still required substantial compensation, but its relative resilience indicated that Monday was not a new fiscal-led steepening. That structural burden was reinforced later in the session by the government’s latest deficit and interest-cost figures.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Listed credit weakened alongside higher Treasury yields, though lower-quality funds showed less damage than duration-heavy investment grade. LQD fell 0.47%, HYG declined 0.24%, and JNK lost 0.18%. The pattern was compatible with a sharp rise in underlying rates and a selective risk-off session, not a uniform withdrawal from corporate credit. Primary-market activity remained available, but the day’s issuer developments showed that investors increasingly required the right structure, collateral and compensation as benchmark yields and competing supply rose.

AI infrastructure remained the largest structural absorption challenge. Hyperscalers had financed much of their capital spending through unsecured investment-grade bonds, yet investors were approaching single-issuer limits and questioning how much more supply that channel could carry. Data-center bonds broaden the funding base by converting tenant lease payments into collateral, but they shift underwriting toward lease durability, tenant payment capacity, construction completion, power access and uptime. Moving beyond the hyperscalers’ corporate balance sheets therefore expands capacity at a higher analytical and pricing hurdle.

The growth of hedging capacity confirmed that investors were managing a larger and more differentiated technology-credit exposure. Royal Bank of Canada had begun making markets in dollar single-name credit-default swaps and planned to expand into euro contracts. The bank said its June single-name CDS volume almost quadrupled its year-to-date volume, while credit-index option volume doubled from a year earlier. New swaps and baskets created as issuers enter the bond market can improve risk transfer, yet the demand for them also reflects a market less willing to treat every large AI borrower as interchangeable high-grade duration.

Securitization offered another route around unsecured corporate balance sheets. Robinhood was gauging demand for at least $400 million of credit-card receivables bonds in four parts, with potential size of $500 million and initial talk near 80 basis points over the benchmark for the highest-rated tranche. Wells Fargo and Barclays were running the transaction, while Coastal Community Bank owned the underlying branded-card accounts. More than $11 billion of card-backed securities had been raised during the year, including Capital One’s $3.85 billion sale, showing that consumer receivables still attracted dedicated financing.

Sterling credit illustrated access with issuer-level conditions attached. Pinewood held investor meetings for a possible fixed-rate bond, with ratings split between BB- at S&P and BBB- at Fitch. Its existing capital structure included a £750 million 6% note due in 2030 and a £300 million note maturing in November 2027. The marketing process showed that a familiar operating asset could approach the market even during a rates and geopolitical selloff, while the ratings split and near-term maturity kept pricing dependent on credit work beyond the strength of the brand.

Credit ended the session open but more segmented. Total returns were pressured by Treasuries, technology borrowing was generating both structural alternatives and new hedging demand, and securitized markets offered capacity beyond conventional bonds. None of those channels removed the underlying constraint from higher base rates. They redistributed it among unsecured issuers, project and tenant risk, receivable pools, and specific maturity profiles. The modest high-yield fund declines kept broad funding stress contained, even as the financing architecture became more complex and the cost of absorbing new debt rose.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Japan reversed the prior close’s most supportive global-duration signal. The earlier call for public pensions to invest more at home had lifted JGBs and the yen, but Monday brought a report that no GPIF allocation overhaul was planned. The yen weakened as much as 0.4% to 162.36 per dollar and JGBs extended losses. The $1.8 trillion fund had retained equal quarter allocations to domestic bonds, domestic equities, foreign bonds and foreign equities at its March 2025 review. Any strategic change requires a formal fiduciary process, limiting the prospect of rapid politically directed demand for domestic duration.

The reversal reopened two transmission risks for Treasuries. Without a prompt GPIF shift, Japan’s long end still faces pressure from ambitious public spending, a weak currency and concern that BOJ normalization is lagging inflation. Higher JGB yields raise the relative return hurdle for global sovereign bonds. At the same time, leaving the allocation unchanged avoids an immediate reduction in the fund’s foreign-asset target. The near-term effect was therefore higher global term pressure through JGB losses, with the longer-run direction of Japanese overseas demand still unresolved.

Europe carried a stagflationary mix rather than a clean duration signal. Economists cut their median 2026 euro-area growth forecast to 0.5% from 0.7%, below the ECB’s 0.8% baseline, as renewed Hormuz disruption damaged expectations for energy supply. Their inflation forecast remained 2.8%, well above the ECB’s target, and they expected another rate increase in September. Weaker growth would ordinarily support sovereign duration, but energy-driven inflation and the prospect of additional tightening preserved a high real-rate hurdle and limited the relief that slower activity could transmit to Treasuries.

The US fiscal update strengthened the structural supply backdrop after its 2 p.m. ET release. June recorded a $120.3 billion deficit after a $27.0 billion surplus a year earlier, as receipts fell and outlays rose. The fiscal-year-to-date deficit widened to $1.367 trillion, net interest reached $827.2 billion, and borrowing from the public totaled $1.340 trillion. These figures arrived too late to explain the earlier rates selloff. They did reinforce the amount of sovereign funding and interest expense embedded in the long-end clearing rate during the final hours of trading.

Global duration consequently transmitted pressure through different mechanisms. Japan removed the prospect of an immediate buyer-base rescue, Europe paired weaker growth with above-target inflation, and the US fiscal statement underscored continuing borrowing needs. The Treasury curve’s belly-led move showed that Fed repricing remained the primary daily force. The 30-year yield above 5% nevertheless reflected a world in which public balance sheets, energy security and domestic pension mandates compete for duration capital even when auctions clear in an orderly way.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Emerging-market debt faced a harsher common backdrop than at the prior close. EMB fell 0.64% as the US Dollar Index rose 0.31% to 101.28 and the 10-year Treasury real yield increased. The simultaneous rise in the dollar and US discount rate tightened conditions for hard-currency borrowers, while the oil shock divided importers from exporters. Index weakness did not erase country-specific support, but it increased the value of fiscal credibility, reserve depth and active maturity management.

Indonesia received rating relief without a full market reversal. S&P affirmed the sovereign at BBB with a stable outlook, citing the 3% of GDP deficit ceiling and a history of fiscal discipline. The decision removed an immediate downgrade threat after Moody’s and Fitch had moved their outlooks to negative. Indonesia’s 10-year dollar bond still traded 0.4 cents lower at 100.9, while local bonds recovered part of their losses and equities closed higher. Planned cuts to the free-meals budget supported the fiscal anchor, but a rupiah down almost 8% during the year kept external confidence and imported inflation central.

Chile showed how tight spreads can coexist with a larger borrowing requirement. Its dollar spread had narrowed 24 basis points since the end of March to 82, near levels last seen in 2007. The Senate authorized an additional $6.2 billion of borrowing, and the Finance Ministry planned $5.2 billion of international issuance over the rest of the year after seeking 36% more debt than initially planned. With economic activity contracting year over year for five months and the structural-balance target relaxed, strong access was compensating for fiscal slippage. Near-total dependence on imported energy made the Hormuz shock an added execution risk.

Kenya’s proposed liability management addressed rollover timing more than debt capacity. Officials were considering buying back as much as $500 million of eurobonds during the fiscal year ending June 2027 and financing the repurchase with new dollar debt. It would be the fourth such exercise in two years. External debt totaled $43.7 billion at the end of March, including $10.6 billion owed to eurobond investors, while debt service exceeded spending on health and education. Extending maturities can smooth the near-term profile, but repeated exchanges leave market access and revenue mobilization as the binding constraints.

Local policy responses to inflation were becoming more restrictive. Ethiopia raised its benchmark rate to 16% from 15%, the first increase since the rate was introduced, while removing a 24% annual cap on bank credit growth. Inflation had risen to 13.4% in May, and the central bank expected it to remain in double digits over the next six months. India’s June CPI accelerated to 4.38% from 3.93%, led partly by food inflation rising to 5.32% from 4.78%; rural inflation at 4.74% exceeded the 3.92% urban rate. Both cases put a firmer floor under local sovereign yields despite different credit and growth structures.

Nigeria added a prospective version of the same energy and expectations channel. Standard Chartered raised its 2026 inflation forecast to 15.5% from 12% and saw only 150 basis points of easing this year, which would leave the policy rate at 25% from 26.5% currently. The central bank’s inflation-perception index rose to 45 from 44.8, with energy, insecurity, borrowing costs and the exchange rate identified as key pressures. The forecast was not a policy decision, but it showed how renewed fuel costs could slow easing where expectations were already difficult to anchor.

The EM close combined a common dollar-rate shock with sharply different domestic buffers. Indonesia preserved investment grade, Chile retained unusually cheap external access, and Kenya sought to exchange near-term maturities for a longer profile. Ethiopia tightened, while firmer Indian inflation and revised Nigerian forecasts constrained local duration. The broad selloff therefore carried both global and sovereign content: higher US real yields raised the base cost, and each borrower’s fiscal rule, energy exposure and refinancing structure determined how strongly that cost transmitted.

## Cross asset

Oil was the principal bridge from geopolitics to fixed income. WTI rose 9.42% to $78.14 and Brent gained 9.59% to $83.30 after renewed strikes and the announcement of a 20% charge on cargo crossing Hormuz. At prevailing prices, the proposed payment was estimated at about $32 million for a fully loaded very large crude carrier, far above previously described Iranian tolls. The proposal’s implementation was unclear, but its potential to raise shipping and fuel costs was enough to revive inflation concern during Monday’s session.

Equities and volatility showed that the shock reached growth assets without producing indiscriminate liquidation. The S&P 500 fell 0.79%, the Nasdaq 100 declined 1.88%, and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index dropped 4.78%. The VIX rose 14.17% to 17.16. Technology bore the larger loss as the South Korean chip selloff spilled into US trading, including a 9.3% decline in SK Hynix’s American depositary shares. The combination of weaker stocks and cheaper Treasuries was consistent with an inflationary discount-rate shock, with the semiconductor move adding sector-specific pressure.

The dollar and gold reinforced the real-rate side of the thesis. The dollar index advanced to 101.28, increasing the external financing burden for emerging-market borrowers. Gold futures fell 2.61% to $3,997 even as geopolitical tension intensified. Higher real yields and a firmer dollar outweighed the metal’s haven role, distinguishing the session from a conventional fear-driven rally in both Treasuries and bullion. The decline also followed Waller’s warning that persistent core inflation could elicit a policy response.

Cross assets therefore confirmed both parts of the fixed-income move. Oil and rising volatility carried the inflation shock, while technology weakness showed that higher discount rates and regional AI stress were hitting risk assets. The stronger dollar and lower gold price aligned with higher real yields. High-yield credit declined far less than semiconductors and volatility remained below crisis levels, leaving the unresolved issue as persistence: a durable Hormuz cost shock could broaden the repricing, while normalization would expose how much of Monday’s move came from Waller’s underlying-inflation concern.

## Forward Market Significance

June CPI and real earnings at 8:30 a.m. ET on July 14, followed by Chair Kevin Warsh’s House testimony at 10:00 a.m., are the immediate test of the policy path; June PPI on July 15 and Warsh’s Senate appearance extend the same thread. Broad cooling in core and services inflation, firmer real earnings and a chair willing to emphasize patience would challenge Monday’s rise in July hike pricing and relieve the two- to five-year sector. Persistent core pressure, weak real-income growth or an explicit readiness to tighten would confirm Waller’s warning that energy is amplifying an inflation problem already present before the latest strikes. Front-end yields, real rates, breakevens, the dollar and event volatility will show whether the close was a durable reaction-function reset or a high-conviction move awaiting validation.

The $95 billion 42-day bill auction at 11:30 a.m. ET on July 14, the settlement of Monday’s 91- and 182-day sales on July 16, and the H.4.1 liquidity release at 4:30 p.m. that day form one cash-absorption sequence. Strong auction coverage, broad nondealer demand, stable secured financing and ample reserves through settlement would confirm that Monday’s outer-bill cheapening reflected policy expectations. Dealer-heavy demand, firmer funding rates or a material reserve drain as Treasury rebuilds its cash balance would reopen a liquidity premium at the front end. Aquarion Water Authority’s planned $2.4 billion sale on July 14 adds a separate public-credit observation within the same absorption theme: reception for its A- senior and BBB+ subordinate debt will show whether a large new municipal name can fund a completed acquisition without disturbing an otherwise orderly primary market.

Japan’s roughly ¥700 billion 20-year JGB auction on July 14 is the clearest near-term test of the global buyer-base reversal, with euro-area industrial production on July 15 providing the growth side of the European duration signal. Strong JGB demand, stable post-auction trading and a firmer yen would challenge Monday’s loss of confidence in immediate domestic absorption, even if GPIF’s strategic allocation remains unchanged. Weak bidding, renewed super-long cheapening or further yen depreciation would confirm that fiscal supply and delayed BOJ normalization still require a higher term premium, with Treasury long yields sensitive through relative value and hedging costs. Weak European output could add duration support; resilience alongside persistent energy inflation would preserve the higher global real-rate hurdle and leave the US long end exposed despite the belly-led nature of Monday’s selloff.

section_summaries.market_read: The close was a policy-path and real-rate shock, with liquidity still functioning normally.
section_summaries.next_check: Core inflation and Warsh’s testimony must show whether the tightening repricing can persist.
section_summaries.summary: Renewed Hormuz conflict and Waller’s warning converted an energy shock into a higher expected policy path, concentrating Treasury losses in the front end and belly. Real yields drove most of the move, while orderly bill absorption and limited high-yield damage contained systemic funding concerns.

section_summaries.market_read: Belly underperformance signals a restrictive-path reset rather than a fiscal-led steepening.
section_summaries.next_check: The next core print must distinguish a durable hike discussion from a one-session repricing.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Treasury cheapening peaked in the five-year sector as imminent-hike risk met expectations of prolonged restraint. Real yields contributed more than breakevens, while strong bill auctions and abundant reserves separated hawkish policy repricing from immediate funding stress.

section_summaries.market_read: Corporate funding remains available, but higher base rates are redistributing risk across structures.
section_summaries.next_check: Reception for new unsecured and securitized deals must show whether segmentation remains orderly.
section_summaries.summary: Credit stayed open but became more segmented as rising Treasury yields pressured duration-heavy investment grade more than high yield. AI financing shifted toward collateralized structures and new hedges, while securitization and selective issuance preserved capacity at higher underwriting and pricing hurdles.

section_summaries.market_read: The long end retains a structural supply premium even when daily losses are led by the belly.
section_summaries.next_check: Japan’s 20-year auction must test whether domestic demand can restrain global term pressure.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Global duration lost support as Japan ruled out an imminent pension-allocation shift, Europe combined weaker growth with energy-driven inflation, and the US fiscal update reinforced borrowing pressure. Fed repricing remained the day’s main force, but elevated public funding needs sustained the long-end clearing hurdle.

section_summaries.market_read: EM duration now carries both a higher global discount rate and sharper country-specific penalties.
section_summaries.next_check: Dollar strength, energy costs and refinancing execution must reveal which sovereign buffers hold.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: A firmer dollar and higher US real yields tightened conditions for emerging-market debt, with the oil shock further separating importers from exporters. Indonesia’s fiscal anchor, Chile’s market access and Kenya’s liability management offered buffers, but refinancing structures and local inflation kept sovereign outcomes differentiated.

section_summaries.market_read: Cross assets confirm an inflationary real-rate shock rather than a conventional haven episode.
section_summaries.next_check: Hormuz costs must either persist and broaden repricing or normalize and expose the core-inflation driver.
section_summaries.summary: Oil transmitted geopolitical risk into inflation expectations, while technology losses and higher volatility showed the discount-rate shock spreading through risk assets. A stronger dollar and weaker gold confirmed the dominance of real yields, yet modest high-yield losses and sub-crisis volatility argued against indiscriminate liquidation.

section_summaries.market_read: The selloff needs confirmation across policy expectations, cash absorption and global duration.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch the two-to-five-year sector, secured funding and long yields through the scheduled tests.
section_summaries.summary: Three near-term tests will determine whether Monday’s repricing endures: inflation data and Warsh’s testimony, the bill-settlement liquidity sequence, and Japan’s long-bond auction. The key distinction is whether policy, funding and global term-premium channels validate the move or reveal a high-conviction close awaiting confirmation.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.36% (+4 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.26% (+2 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 36 bp (+1 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 84 bp (-1 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Oil shock and tighter Fed path drive a belly-led Treasury selloff
overall_summary.session_narrative: The July 13 NY close extended the restrictive-rate regime, with the five-year sector leading losses as oil revived headline-inflation risk and Waller made near-term tightening a live possibility. Higher real yields carried most of the nominal repricing, while a firmer dollar and weaker risk assets spread the pressure beyond Treasuries. Robust bill demand, abundant cash and relatively modest high-yield losses kept the move from becoming a liquidity or systemic credit event.
overall_summary.summary: Treasuries cheapened across the curve as renewed Hormuz risk and Waller’s inflation warning raised the expected policy path. Higher real yields carried most of the move, while strong bill demand and soft funding kept market function orderly.

watch_events.market_implication: Cooling would relieve the front end; firmness would validate higher real yields, the dollar and event risk.
watch_events.market_trigger: Broad core and services cooling plus patient guidance, or persistent pressure and readiness to tighten.
watch_events.title: Inflation data and Warsh testimony
watch_events.watch_line: CPI, real earnings, PPI and Warsh’s testimony will test the near-term policy path.

watch_events.market_implication: Orderly absorption supports a policy repricing; strain would add funding and public-credit premia.
watch_events.market_trigger: Broad nondealer demand, stable funding and a well-received Aquarion sale, or signs of balance-sheet strain.
watch_events.title: Cash and primary-market absorption
watch_events.watch_line: The bill sequence, reserve data and Aquarion sale will test cash and primary-market absorption.

watch_events.market_implication: Strength would ease term pressure; weakness would keep Treasury long yields exposed through relative value.
watch_events.market_trigger: Strong JGB demand and a firmer yen, or weak bidding, renewed cheapening and further depreciation.
watch_events.title: Japan long-duration demand
watch_events.watch_line: Japan’s 20-year auction and euro-area output will test buyer demand and the global real-rate hurdle.
